Transformer Architecture Explained (Step-by-Step):

that's the deal i've made with you.
now today's topic is one you've been asking about non-stop since my last article dropped.

"can you explain Transformer Architecture?"
so here we are.
this is the one that powers ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Google Translate... basically every AI tool you use daily.
and by the time you finish reading this, you'll actually understand how it works.
not surface level. a real understanding.
let's get into it.

Why Understanding Language Models Matters

You don't need to be an AI researcher to benefit from understanding this.
Every time you use a chatbot, run an AI writing tool, or get a smart autocomplete suggestion... a language model is doing the work behind the scenes.
Understanding how these models think helps you use them better.
It also helps you spot the hype from the substance when a new AI tool drops.
Most people treat AI as a black box. You're about to be different.

A Simple Overview of Neural Networks

image source: wiki pedia
Before we get to Transformers, you need a quick picture of what a neural network is.
Think of a neural network like an orchestra.
You have many different sections: strings, brass, woodwinds, percussion.
Each section handles a specific part of the music.
None of them play the full song alone. But together, they produce something complete.
A neural network works the same way.
It has layers. Each layer processes a specific part of the data and passes its result to the next layer.
By the time the data reaches the final layer, the network has built a full understanding of what it received.
That's it. That's a neural network.
Simple, right? Now let's talk about how AI used to handle text before Transformers existed.

How Early AI Models Worked with Text

Before Transformers, AI used something called an RNN (Recurrent Neural Network).
The way an RNN reads text is exactly how the name sounds.
It reads one word at a time. Then it remembers what it read. Then it moves to the next word.
Imagine reading a book by reading one word, pausing, writing a note about that word, then moving to the next.
Word by word. Note by note. All the way to the end.
That's how RNNs processed language.
And for a while, it worked. RNNs could translate sentences, predict the next word in a sequence, and handle basic language tasks.
But then people started feeding them longer, more complex text.
And things started breaking.

Problems with Older Text Models

RNNs had two massive problems.
Problem 1: They forgot things.
Imagine you're playing a game of telephone. You whisper a message to someone, they whisper it to the next person, and so on down a long chain.
By the time the message reaches the last person... it's completely different from what you started with.
That's exactly what happens to RNNs with long text.
Information passed from word to word gets weaker with every step.
By the time the model reaches the end of a long sentence, it's already forgotten what was at the beginning.
This is called the vanishing gradient problem. And it made RNNs terrible at understanding long text.
Problem 2: They were painfully slow.
Because RNNs read one word at a time, they couldn't process multiple words at once.
Your computer's GPU is built for doing many things in parallel. But RNNs couldn't use that power because they were locked into reading sequentially.
Processing a single sentence took forever. Processing an entire book? Don't even think about it.
So researchers started asking a very important question.
What if we could build something that doesn't read word by word? What if we could look at all words at once?
That question led to the invention that changed everything.

The Encoder-Decoder Idea (Before Transformers)

scss
// Stage 1: Encoder (Reading & Compressing)
[French Sentence: "Le chat est sur le tapis"]
      |
      v
[RNN Encoder] --> (Processes word-by-word) --> [Compressed Meaning Vector: "cat, on, mat"]

// Stage 2: Decoder (Unpacking & Generating)
[Compressed Meaning Vector]
      |
      v
[RNN Decoder] --> (Generates word-by-word) --> [English Sentence: "The cat is on the mat"]

** But... both were slow RNNs **
Before Transformers existed, there was already a useful structure called the encoder-decoder.
You can think of it like a translator working in two stages.
>Stage 1 (Encoder): Read the original French sentence and understand it fully. >Stage 2 (Decoder): Take that understanding and produce the English translation.
The encoder's job is to read and compress.
The decoder's job is to take that compressed understanding and produce something new.
This structure made a lot of sense. And it worked.
But there was a catch.
Both the encoder and decoder in these early models were still built with RNNs underneath.
So they inherited all the same problems. Slow. Forgetful. Limited.
The structure was good. The engine was broken.
Researchers needed a new engine.

Why Translation and Language Understanding Were Hard

Here's a sentence that shows why language is so tricky:
"The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired."
What does "it" refer to? The animal or the street?
You know immediately. The animal. Streets don't get tired.
But an AI model has to figure this out by connecting "it" all the way back to "animal" across several words in between.
For an RNN reading one word at a time... that connection gets lost.
The model might assume "it" refers to the street because the street was the most recently mentioned noun.
This is the kind of mistake that makes translations sound broken and answers feel off.
Language is full of these long-distance connections.
Pronouns. Clauses. References. Context from earlier in the paragraph.
Handling all of this correctly requires a model that can look at the full picture all at once.
Not word by word. All at once.
That's exactly what Transformers were designed to do.

The Attention Mechanism: The Big Idea

scss

  Instead of reading word-by-word...
  What if every word could look at EVERY other word
  and decide which ones matter most?

  Sentence: "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired"

  For the word "it", attention assigns scores:

  Word:      [The]  [animal]  [didn't]  [cross]  [street]  [it]  [tired]
                                                             ^
  Score:      3%      87%       1%        1%        3%     self    8%
              |        |                             |              |
              |        |                             |              |
              dim    BRIGHT                         dim           some
           (drown)  (keep!)                       (drown)       (note)

  The model KEEPS the info from "animal" (87% weight)
  and DROWNS OUT irrelevant words (near 0% weight)

  Result: "it" now carries the meaning of "animal" inside it.
  The model correctly knows "it" = the animal. Not the street.

  This is attention. A spotlight on what matters.
In 2017, a team at Google published a paper with a bold title.
Attention Is All You Need.
The big idea was this:
>What if instead of reading words one by one... the model could look at every word at the same time and decide which words matter most for understanding each other word?
This is called attention.
And the best way to understand it is with a simple example.
When you read the sentence "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired"...
Your brain doesn't read it word by word and forget what came before.
You hold the whole sentence in your head. And when you hit "it"... you look back, find "animal," and connect them.
That looking back and connecting is exactly what attention does.
For every word in a sentence, attention asks three questions:
source image:
d2l.ai
>Query: What is this word looking for? >Key: What does each other word offer? >Value: What is the actual content of each word
The model compares the Query of one word against the Keys of all other words.
The closer the match, the more "attention" gets paid to that word.
It's like a spotlight. Words that are relevant to each other get lit up. Words that don't matter get dimmed.
This is how the model figures out that "it" belongs to "animal" and not "street."

What Is a Transformer Model

scss
[Input Sentence]
      |
      v
+-------------+
| Layer 6     | --+
+-------------+   |
| Layer 5     |   |
+-------------+   |--> [Encoder Stack: Reads & Understands]
| ...         |   |
+-------------+   |
| Layer 1     | --+
+-------------+
      |
      +-----> (Rich, Contextual Understanding is passed to every Decoder layer)
      |
      v
+-------------+
| Layer 6     | --+
+-------------+   |
| Layer 5     |   |
+-------------+   |--> [Decoder Stack: Writes & Generates]
| ...         |   |
+-------------+   |
| Layer 1     | --+
+-------------+
      |
      v
[Output Sentence]
A Transformer is a model that uses attention as its core engine.
No recurrence. No reading word by word. Just attention.
Every word looks at every other word. All at once.
The original Transformer was built for machine translation. You give it a sentence in one language and it gives you back a sentence in another language.
But the insight was so powerful that it became the foundation for nearly every major AI model that exists today.
GPT. BERT. Claude. Gemini. All of them trace their roots back to this one paper published by eight researchers at Google.

Why Transformers Changed Everything

Before Transformers, training a state-of-the-art translation model required months of compute and enormous cost.
The original Transformer model was trained in 12 hours on 8 GPUs.
And it beat every previous model that existed. Including models that had taken months to train.
Not by a little. By a lot.
It scored 28.4 on a translation benchmark called BLEU for English to German.
The best previous model scored 26.3.
And the Transformer did it at a fraction of the cost.
The reason it was so much faster is simple.
Attention processes all words in parallel. RNNs processed them one at a time.
Using modern GPUs designed for parallel computation, Transformers could do in hours what took other models weeks.
Speed plus performance. That combination doesn't happen often in AI.

High-Level View of Transformer Architecture

Here's the picture you need in your head.
A Transformer has two main parts.
>The Encoder stack: Reads and understands the input. >The Decoder stack: Produces the output based on that understanding.
Each stack is made up of 6 identical layers stacked on top of each other.
Think of it like a 6-floor building.
The input enters on the ground floor. Each floor processes and refines the understanding a little more. By the time it exits the top floor, the model has a deep, rich understanding of the input.
The output from the encoder then gets handed to the decoder.
The decoder takes that understanding and builds the output one word at a time.
That's the bird's-eye view. Now let's zoom in on each part.

How Transformers Read Input Text

scss
      "How are you?"

  STEP 1: TOKENIZE
  Break into pieces the model knows
  "How are you?" --> [How] [are] [you] [?]
                      T1    T2    T3   T4
  Vocabulary size: ~37,000 tokens (byte-pair encoding)

  STEP 2: EMBED
  Each token -> a vector of 512 numbers
  capturing its meaning mathematically

  [How] --> [0.2, 0.8, -0.3, 0.6, 0.1, ... ] (512 numbers)
  [are] --> [0.5, 0.3,  0.7, 0.1, 0.9, ... ] (512 numbers)
  [you] --> [0.1, 0.6,  0.2, 0.8, 0.4, ... ] (512 numbers)

  "king" and "queen" have SIMILAR vectors (close in meaning)
  "king" and "banana" have DIFFERENT vectors (far apart)

  STEP 3: POSITIONAL ENCODING
  Problem: attention sees all words at once — loses word order!
  "Dog bites man" ≠ "Man bites dog" (same words, diff meaning)

  Solution: add a position signal to each embedding
  using sine/cosine waves of different frequencies

  [How, pos=1] = embedding(How) + PE(1)
  [are, pos=2] = embedding(are) + PE(2)
  [you, pos=3] = embedding(you) + PE(3)

  Now the model knows WHAT each word means AND WHERE it sits.
Before any attention can happen, the Transformer needs to convert your text into numbers.
Here's how it works:
Step 1: Tokenization
Your sentence gets broken into small pieces called tokens.
A token is usually a word or part of a word.
"Understanding" might become two tokens: "Under" and "standing."
This is just how the model breaks language into chunks it can process.
Step 2: Embeddings
Each token gets converted into a list of 512 numbers.
This list is called a vector or embedding.
These numbers capture the meaning of the word. Words that mean similar things end up with similar numbers.
"King" and "Queen" will have vectors that are close to each other. "King" and "banana" will not.
Step 3: Positional Encoding
Here's the problem with attention.
When you look at all words at once, you lose track of their order.
"Dog bites man" and "Man bites dog" have the same words. But very different meanings.
So the Transformer adds positional encoding to each word's vector.
Think of it like numbering the seats in a theater. Every word gets a seat number.
This way, the model knows not just what each word means, but where it appeared in the sentence.
Now the model is ready to start paying attention.

What Is Self-Attention (Explained Simply)

scss
// Sentence: "The cat sat"

// Before Self-Attention:
Vector for "cat" = [0.5, 0.3, 0.9, ...]  (Represents just the word "cat")

// During Self-Attention:
(Vector for "cat" looks at "The" and "sat")
New Vector for "cat" = (30% of "The"s Value) + (60% of "cat"s Value) + (10% of "sat"s Value)

// After Self-Attention:
Vector for "cat" = [0.8, 0.2, 0.6, ...]  (Now represents "cat" in the context of "The cat sat")
Self-attention is the part that makes Transformers so powerful.
Here's the simplest version of how it works.
For each word in your sentence, the model creates three things:
>A Query vector: What is this word looking for? >A Key vector: What does this word offer to others? >A Value vector: What is the actual content of this word?
Then for each word, the model compares its Query against the Key of every other word.
The comparison produces a score. A high score means "these two words are relevant to each other."
Those scores get turned into percentages using a process called softmax.
So for the word "it" in our earlier example... "animal" might score 87% relevance and "street" might score 3%.
The model then combines all the word values using those percentages as weights.
The result is a new, richer understanding of each word... shaped by its relationship to every other word.
This is why it's called self-attention. The sentence is paying attention to itself.

Why Attention Is Better Than Memory

scss
// RNN: A Weakening Chain
[Word 1] -> [Word 2] -> [Word 3] -> [Word 4] -> [Word 5]
(Signal from Word 1 is very weak by the time it reaches Word 5)

// Attention: Direct Connections
      +----------+----------+
      |          |          |
[Word 1] -- [Word 2] -- [Word 3] -- [Word 4] -- [Word 5]
   |          |                        |          |
   +----------+------------------------+----------+
(Signal from Word 1 to Word 5 is direct and full strength)
This is the key comparison you need to understand.
RNNs relied on memory. They passed information from word to word like a chain.
Attention doesn't rely on memory. It makes direct connections.
Here's the difference in practice.
With an RNN, for "it" to connect to "animal" across 7 words... the signal has to pass through 7 steps.
Each step weakens the signal. By step 7, most of the "animal" information is gone.
With attention, "it" connects directly to "animal" in one step.
It doesn't matter how far apart they are. One step. Every time.
That's why attention is so much better at handling long-range connections.
And because all words are processed at once instead of one by one... everything is also much faster.

The Encoder: What It Does

scss
   The Encoder reads and understands the input.
   6 identical layers stacked. Each layer = same 2 sub-layers.

  [Input: word vectors + positional encoding]
           |
           v
  +----------------------------------------------+
  |  SUB-LAYER 1: Multi-Head Self-Attention       |
  |                                               |
  |  Every word attends to every other word.      |
  |  8 heads. dk=64 each. All in parallel.        |
  |                                               |
  |  "The" looks at "cat", "sat", "mat"...        |
  |  "cat" looks at "The", "sat", "mat"...        |
  |  All simultaneously.                          |
  +--------+-------------------------------------+
           |
           + <-- residual (skip connection adds original input back)
           |
  [Add & LayerNorm]  <- stabilizes training, prevents vanishing gradients
           |
           v
  +----------------------------------------------+
  |  SUB-LAYER 2: Feed-Forward Network            |
  |                                               |
  |  Linear (512 -> 2048) -> ReLU -> Linear (2048 -> 512)
  |                                               |
  |  Applied to EACH WORD independently.          |
  |  Same weights, different inputs.              |
  |  Adds non-linearity and depth.                |
  +--------+-------------------------------------+
           |
           + <-- residual (skip connection)
           |
  [Add & LayerNorm]
           |
           v
  [Output: richer word vectors, passed to next layer]

  After 6 of these layers: deep, context-aware representation.
The encoder is the part of the Transformer that reads and understands your input.
Each of the 6 encoder layers does two things:
>First: Runs self-attention on all the words so they can understand each other. >Second: Passes each word through a small neural network to refine its representation.
After self-attention, each word's vector has been updated to include context from every other word.
After the neural network, the representation gets sharpened further.
Then the result gets passed to the next encoder layer above it.
After 6 layers of this... the encoder has built a deep, rich, context-aware understanding of the entire input.
That understanding gets passed to the decoder.
One important thing: all of this happens in parallel.
All words, all at once, across all 6 layers.
No waiting. No sequential bottleneck. Just pure parallel processing.

The Decoder: What It Does

scss
   The Decoder generates the output. One word at a time.
   6 identical layers. Each has 3 sub-layers (not 2).

  [Previous output words + positional encoding]
           |
           v
  +----------------------------------------------+
  |  SUB-LAYER 1: MASKED Multi-Head Self-Attention|
  |                                               |
  |  Like encoder self-attention BUT:             |
  |  future positions are MASKED (set to -inf)    |
  |                                               |
  |  Generating word 3?                           |
  |  Can only see words 1 and 2. NOT word 4+.     |
  |  No peeking at the answer. No cheating.       |
  +--------+-------------------------------------+
           |
  [Add & LayerNorm]
           |
           v
  +----------------------------------------------+
  |  SUB-LAYER 2: Encoder-Decoder CROSS-Attention |
  |                                               |
  |  Q  <-- from decoder (what am I generating?) |
  |  K, V <-- from ENCODER output (the input)    |
  |                                               |
  |  This is the BRIDGE. The decoder looks at    |
  |  the full encoded input and asks:            |
  |  "which parts of the input matter right now?"|
  +--------+-------------------------------------+
           |
  [Add & LayerNorm]
           |
           v
  +----------------------------------------------+
  |  SUB-LAYER 3: Feed-Forward Network            |
  |  (same as encoder: 512->2048->512)            |
  +--------+-------------------------------------+
           |
  [Add & LayerNorm]
           |
           v
  [Linear layer -> 37,000 scores, one per vocab word]
           |
           v
  [Softmax -> probabilities -> pick highest -> output word]
The decoder is where the output gets created.
It also has 6 layers. But each layer does three things instead of two.
>First: It runs self-attention on the words it has already produced (so it understands its own output so far). >Second: It looks at the encoder's output to understand the original input. >Third: It passes through a small neural network to refine everything.
That second step is crucial.
This is called cross-attention. The decoder looks at the encoder's full understanding of the input and asks "which parts of the input are most relevant to what I'm producing right now?"
If you're translating "The cat sat on the mat" and you're currently generating the word "cat" in French... the decoder will pay high attention to the word "cat" in the encoder's representation.
It's a direct, intelligent bridge between understanding the input and producing the output.
After the 6th decoder layer, the output goes through one final step.
The model produces a score for every word in its vocabulary.
The word with the highest score gets chosen as the next output token.
Then that token gets fed back in, and the process repeats until the full output is complete.

How Encoder and Decoder Work Together

scss
       Full pipeline. Start to finish.

  [Input: "How are you?"]
          |
          v
  (1) Tokenize + Embed + Add Positional Encoding
          |
          v
  (2) ENCODER (6 layers, runs fully in parallel)
      Layer 1: Self-Attention + FFN
      Layer 2: Self-Attention + FFN
      ...
      Layer 6: Self-Attention + FFN
          |
          v (produces K, V vectors for every input position)
          |
          +-------> K, V sent to EVERY decoder layer
          |
          v
  (3) DECODER (6 layers, runs one step at a time)
      Starts with <START> token
      Layer 1: Masked Self-Attn + Cross-Attn(K,V from encoder) + FFN
      Layer 2: same...
      ...
      Layer 6: same...
          |
          v
  (4) Linear + Softmax -> pick top word -> e.g., "¿Cómo"
          |
          v
  (5) Feed "¿Cómo" back as new decoder input
          |
          v
  (6) Decoder runs again -> picks "estás"
          |
          v
  (7) Repeat until <END> token is generated
          |
          v
  [Output: "¿Cómo estás?"]

  The encoder runs ONCE. The decoder loops until done.
Here's the full picture end to end.
>Step 1: Your sentence gets tokenized and converted into vectors. >Step 2: Positional encodings are added so the model knows word order. >Step 3: The vectors pass through the 6 encoder layers. Each layer refines understanding using self-attention and a small neural network. >Step 4: The encoder's final output is a rich, context-aware representation of every word in your input. >Step 5: The decoder receives that representation. It starts generating output one word at a time. >Step 6: For each new word, the decoder looks at what it's already generated (self-attention) and what the encoder understood (cross-attention). >Step 7: The most likely next word gets selected and added to the output. >Step 8: Repeat until the output is complete.
That's the full Transformer pipeline.
Input goes in. Understanding gets built. Output comes out.
Clean, parallel, and incredibly powerful.

Why Transformers Are Faster and Smarter

scss
     Parallelism changes everything.

  RNN processing 6 words:
  t=1: process "The"    (GPU waits)
  t=2: process "cat"    (GPU waits)
  t=3: process "sat"    (GPU waits)
  t=4: process "on"     (GPU waits)
  t=5: process "the"    (GPU waits)
  t=6: process "mat"    (GPU now outputs)
  Total time: 6 sequential steps.

  Transformer processing 6 words:
  t=1: process ALL 6 WORDS simultaneously
  Total time: 1 step.
  GPU fully used. No waiting.

  Real training times:
  GNMT + RL:            weeks      | 1.8 × 10^20 FLOPs
  Transformer base:     12 hours   | 3.3 × 10^18 FLOPs (7x fewer!)
  Transformer big:      3.5 days   | 2.3 × 10^19 FLOPs

  SMARTER: Better long-range understanding.

  RNN connecting word 1 to word 50:
  50 sequential hops. Signal degrades. Meaning lost.

  Transformer connecting word 1 to word 50:
  1 direct attention step. Full signal. Nothing lost.

  Result: the Transformer understands complex sentences,
  long documents, ambiguous pronouns — that RNNs fumbled.

  Faster training + better results = why the world switched.
Two things make Transformers better than everything that came before.
Faster:
RNNs had to process words one at a time. GPU parallelism was useless to them.
Transformers process all words at once. They are built exactly for the kind of parallel computation GPUs are designed to run.
That's why the original Transformer trained in 12 hours when competing models needed weeks.
Smarter:
RNNs could only see nearby context clearly. Distant words got blurry.
Transformers see everything with equal clarity. Every word connects directly to every other word regardless of distance.
This means the model can understand complex sentences, long documents, subtle references, and ambiguous pronouns in a way RNNs simply couldn't.
More context, captured more accurately, processed more efficiently.
That's the Transformer advantage.

Real-World Examples of Transformer Models

Once Transformers existed, the AI world exploded.
Here are the models you probably already use that are built on this architecture:
>GPT (OpenAI): The engine behind ChatGPT. Decoder-only Transformer trained on massive amounts of text. Used for writing, coding, conversation, analysis.
>BERT (Google): Encoder-only Transformer. Trained to deeply understand text by reading it from both directions. Powers Google Search's understanding of your queries.
>Gemini (Google): Google's latest multimodal Transformer. Handles text, images, video, and code.
All of them are Transformers at the core.
The same fundamental architecture. Different training approaches and data.

Key Advantages of Transformers

Here's a clean summary of why Transformers took over AI.
>Parallel processing: All words handled at once. Not one by one.
>Long-range connections: Every word can connect to every other word directly. Distance doesn't matter.
>Speed: Trained in hours not weeks. Built for modern GPU hardware.
>Performance: Beat every previous model on translation benchmarks when first released.
>Flexibility: Works for text, code, images, video, audio. The architecture is that general.
>Interpretability: You can actually look at the attention scores and see what the model is focusing on. This is something RNNs couldn't offer.
>Scalability: Make the model bigger and it gets smarter. Stack more layers, add more parameters. The architecture scales.
That last point is why we now have models with hundreds of billions of parameters.
The Transformer architecture doesn't break when you scale it. It thrives.

How Everything Fits Together

Let's bring it all home.
Before Transformers, AI read text one word at a time and kept forgetting what it read.
Attention fixed that by letting every word look at every other word directly.
The Transformer is a model built entirely around attention.
It has two parts:
>Encoder: Reads the input and builds a deep understanding of it using self-attention across 6 layers.
>Decoder: Takes that understanding and produces output one word at a time using both self-attention and cross-attention across 6 layers.
The whole thing runs in parallel. No sequential bottleneck. No forgetting.
That's why it trained in 12 hours and beat models that took months to build.
That's why every major AI company adopted it almost immediately.
That's why everything you use in AI today is probably a Transformer.
You just learned something that most people who use AI every day don't actually understand.
Not just the what but the why.
Why Transformers exist. What problem they solved. How attention works. What the encoder and decoder do and how they connect.
That's real understanding.
And real understanding is what separates people who use AI tools from people who can think critically about them, build with them, and explain them to others.
Save this. Come back to it. Share it with someone who's been confused about how AI actually works.
